{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[sn:Tamura Shrine]]

The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] }} [[sn:Tamura Shrine]] [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} [[ja:田村神社 (高松市)]] [[zh:田村神社 (高松市)]]

During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[:en:Gyoki|Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] }} [[sn:Tamura Shrine]] [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} [[ja:田村神社 (高松市)]] [[zh:田村神社 (高松市)]]

During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[:en:Gyoki|Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] }} [[sn:Tamura Shrine]] [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} [[ja:田村神社 (高松市)]] [[zh:田村神社 (高松市)]]

In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Takamatsu, Kagawa|Takamatsu]] in [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[Omononushi]] (the god of [[Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[:en:Gyoki|Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] }} [[sn:Tamura Shrine]] [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} [[ja:田村神社 (高松市)]] [[zh:田村神社 (高松市)]]

The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takamatsu|Takamatsu]] in [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[:en:Omononushi|Omononushi]] (the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[:en:Omononushi|Omononushi]] (the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[:en:Gyoki|Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[:en:Takamatsu Domain|Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[Shikoku pilgrimage]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] }} [[sn:Tamura Shrine]] [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} [[ja:田村神社 (高松市)]] [[zh:田村神社 (高松市)]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Tamura Jinja
田村神社 | image = Tamura shrine20220820 1.jpg | caption = Main Torii of Tamura Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|17|11.53|N|134|1|38.25|E|region:JP-37_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =286 Ichinomiyacho, Takamatsu-shi, Kagawa-ken 761-8084 | website = {{Official|1=https://tamurajinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = May 8 and October 8 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tamura Jinja'''|田村神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takamatsu|Takamatsu]] in [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on May 8 and October 8.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is located approximately 7 km south of downtown Takamatsu. The area has abundant spring water, and the shrine is based on the worship of the god of water and the shrine's inner shrine is built over a spring. The name 'Tamura' is based on locale, and has no relationship with [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] like other shrines of the same name in other parts of Japan. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tamura Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Yamato totohi momoso hime-no-Mikoto'''|倭迹迹日百襲姫命|}}, the daughter of the 7th [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], and a ''[[miko]]'' known for her divine marriage with [[:en:Omononushi|Omononushi]] (the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]) * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 五十狭芹彦命|}}, the son of Emperor Kōrei and conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]]. * {{nihongo|'''[[Sarutahiko Ōkami]]'''|猿田彦大神|}}, leader of the earthly ''kami''. * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Kaguyama no Mikoto'''| 天隠山命|}} * {{nihongo|'''Ame no Ita no Mikoto'''| 天五田根命|}} ==History== The origins of Tamura Jinja are unknown. The monk [[:en:Gyoki|Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). The temple and shrine were patronized by the Imperial family from an early date. The shrine is mentioned in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" and ''[[Engishiki]]'' records from the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from this time.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} It was promoted to Shoichii (Senior First Rank) in 1201, and a tablet with the inscription 'Shoichii Tamura Daimyojin' dated July 1284 survives at the shrine. The shrine was extended and reconstructed by [[:en:Hosokawa Katsumoto|Hosokawa Katsumoto]] in 1460. It was destroyed in the wars of the [[:en:Tenshō (Momoyama period)|Tenshō]] era (1573-1592), but reconstructed again under the patronage of the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira clan]], the [[daimyo]] of [[:en:Takamatsu Domain|Takamatsu Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1679, [[Ichinomiya-ji]] was separated from the Tamura Jinja, and the shrine lost its position as a stop on the [[:en:Shikoku Pilgrimage|Shikoku Pilgrimage]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} *[https://tamurajinja.com Official home page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] }} [[sn:Tamura Shrine]] [[en:Tamura Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu)}} [[ja:田村神社 (高松市)]] [[zh:田村神社 (高松市)]]

The monk [[:en:Gyoki|Gyoki]] built a shrine over a sacred well in 709 AD; however, it is also recorded that a Buddhist temple was built at this site in that [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] era (701-704). During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 10-minute walk from [[:en:Ichinomiya Station|Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad ]] [[Kotoden Kotohira Line ]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) ichinotorii.JPG|Ichi-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) torii.JPG|Ni-no-Torii File:Tamura Jinja, Takamatsu 01.JPG|Zuishinmon Gate File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) shoumen.JPG|San-no-Torii File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Tamurazinzyaɨ0.jpg|Honsha, Okudono File:Tamura-jinja (Takamatsu) keidai.JPG|Precincts ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959).
